steps.title: Use cross-group consistency
steps.content: The team compared odor preferences across ten groups with different modes of living, including urban, agricultural, and hunter-gatherer groups. The important finding is that people generally rated odors about the same across those groups.

steps.title: Draw the limited conclusion
steps.content: Because the ratings were similar across modes of living, the conclusion should say that mode of living likely does not have a large influence on whether a person perceives an odor as pleasant or unpleasant.

steps.title: Reject unsupported conclusions
steps.content:
• A: says culture is dominant, the opposite of what similar cross-cultural ratings suggest.
  • B: follows from the similar ratings across living modes.
  • C: discusses how often people encounter vanillin, which was not measured.
  • D: says pleasant odors are rated equally, but vanillin was rated more pleasant than galbazine.
The answer is B.
